content:
Embedding visualization evolved alongside **multivariate statistics** and **exploratory data analysis**. Early practitioners plotted **principal component scores** (Hotelling-era PCA) on paper as scatter diagrams—color was added manually or via symbol types to mark groups.

The rise of **interactive computing** (1970s–90s) brought rotation of 3D score plots and dynamic brushing: selecting points in one view highlighted metadata in another—a pattern preserved in modern linked-selection tools.

**Non-linear embedding methods** changed the practice, not just the coordinates. **Sammon mapping** (1969) and **MDS** variants prioritized faithful distance layouts; **t-SNE** (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008) made dense, label-colored 2D maps of neural activations iconic in deep learning papers. **UMAP** (McInnes, Healy, Melville, 2018) added scalable layouts widely exported to matplotlib, Plotly, and TensorBoard projector.

Parallel growth in **information visualization** (Bertin, Tufte, Ware) codified encodings—position, color, size—and warned against chartjunk and misleading axes. ML adoption merged these rules with **reproducible research**: saving seeds, embedding arrays, and figure scripts.

Today embedding visualization is a standard **EDA and debugging** layer atop PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, and learned representations—distinct from owning any one reduction algorithm.

timeline.label: PCA score plots
timeline.description: Multivariate analysts scatter first two principal components, marking groups by symbol—early embedding-style EDA.

timeline.label: Interactive 3D rotation
timeline.description: Workstations enable rotating 3D projections and brushing points to inspect metadata.

timeline.label: t-SNE figures in deep learning
timeline.description: Two-dimensional t-SNE maps of hidden layers become a standard paper figure for representation quality.

timeline.label: UMAP and open tooling
timeline.description: Fast UMAP implementations and TensorBoard projector democratize large-scale embedding exploration.

timeline.label: Reproducible viz norms
timeline.description: Community emphasis on saved coordinates, accessible palettes, and explicit method labels in publication figures.
